policy_statement: By 2025, create a public-private facilities managers/building operators roundtable for peer-to-peer learning on fuel switching (BO.3-4).
mechanism_description: The city will create a facilities managers/building operators roundtable to support peer-to-peer learning on fuel switching, helping buildings transition away from fossil fuels and reduce emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Capacity building/peer learning → accelerated fuel switching away from fossil combustion → ↓ direct (and often indirect) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human/system change: Building operators and facilities managers change technology choices and operating practices by adopting electrification/heat pumps or other non-fossil systems sooner and more widely due to shared knowledge, vendor info, and implementation lessons. (2) Mediating process: Reduced combustion of natural gas/fuel oil/propane in boilers, furnaces, and water heaters (and potentially improved controls/commissioning that supports electrification performance). (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions from buildings; net benefit depends on grid emissions but the mechanism’s intent and typical outcome is emissions reduction.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building-sector GHG emissions by accelerating switching from fossil fuels to low/zero-carbon energy for space/water heating and other end uses.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Knowledge-sharing and capacity building leads to fuel switching/electrification, which primarily reduces fossil fuel combustion and thus GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although it can incidentally improve energy efficiency or resilience, the central and largest climate effect of fuel switching programs is emissions mitigation from replacing fossil fuels with low/zero-carbon energy.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the “switch” were to another fossil fuel (e.g., oil→gas) or to biomass with high lifecycle emissions, mitigation could be smaller or ambiguous; if grid electricity is very carbon-intensive, near-term benefits could be reduced, but the dominant intent/effect remains emissions reduction relative to continued fossil combustion in buildings.  
4) Applicability to all instances: Any policy using this mechanism (peer learning focused specifically on fuel switching) is designed to change fuel/technology choices to cut building emissions, making Mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any finer subtype (e.g., “building electrification enabling policy,” “capacity building,” “demand-side decarbonization”) should be assigned at the individual policy level based on evidence of the specific fuels, technologies, and grid context.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be misclassified only if “fuel switching” is defined as switching between fossil fuels for cost/air-quality reasons without net GHG benefit, or if the policy is framed primarily for workforce development rather than emissions; otherwise straightforward mitigation.
key_indicators: “create a Public-Private … roundtable”; “support peer-to-peer learning”; “fuel switching”; “facilities managers and building operators”
co_benefits: Improved building operations/maintenance practices; potential energy cost savings; improved indoor air quality from reduced on-site combustion
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as an information/technical-assistance intervention; classified as planning-strategy because it establishes an ongoing coordination forum rather than a one-off disclosure or training requirement.
